children.name: Hyla
children.description: Small green tree frogs with adhesive toe pads enabling climbing on smooth surfaces. The European Tree Frog is Sweden's rarest amphibian, persisting only in a handful of sites in southernmost Skåne, where it clings to reed stems and calls loudly from pond margins on warm evenings.

children.name: Hyla arborea
children.description: Sweden's rarest amphibian; brilliant green with dark lateral stripe; adhesive toe pads; calls from May to July; IUCN Least Concern but severely declining in northern range.

children.name: Hyla japonica
children.description: Widespread in East Asia; changes colour between green and brown depending on temperature.

children.name: Pseudacris crucifer
children.description: The defining sound of North American spring; only 3 cm long; X-shaped marking on back; chorus audible a kilometre away.

children.name: Pseudacris regilla
children.description: Most frequently heard frog in western North America; Hollywood soundtrack standard.

children.name: Agalychnis callidryas
children.description: The most photographed frog in the world; vivid red eyes, orange feet, and blue flanks; nocturnal; lays eggs on leaves overhanging water — tadpoles drop in on hatching.

children.name: Litoria caerulea
children.description: The iconic dumpy tree frog; calm temperament; deposits lipid on skin to reduce water loss; enormously popular in captivity.

children.name: Litoria infrafrenata
children.description: World's largest tree frog at up to 14 cm; loud barking call.
